nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and coastal reefs, including protected seaward reefs; occasionally enters estuaries. Found at depths of 1–20 m.

decorPreferences: sand substrate for burrowing at night

diet.notes: Has been documented eating small benthic invertebrates, fish eggs, mantis shrimp, and bristle worms. In captivity, tends to accept vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, chopped meaty seafood, and quality marine pellet or flake foods. Generally advised to feed 2–3 times daily given its high metabolic demands.

summary: The Lunare (Lyretail) Wrasse is a striking, active, and highly aggressive Indo-Pacific species best suited to large FOWLR systems. Juveniles are blue with black markings; adults develop vivid green and yellow crescent patterning.
careNotes: Also sold under the trade name 'Lyretail Wrasse'; both names refer to the same species, Thalassoma lunare. Generally considered one of the more boisterous and aggressive wrasses available in the hobby — tends to harass and bully tankmates, particularly new additions, and is generally best introduced last into an established aquarium. Has been known to eat mantis shrimp and bristleworms, which can be advantageous, but also tends to consume beneficial clean-up crew invertebrates. A tightly fitting lid is widely considered essential as this species has been reported to jump frequently. Tends to bury itself in sand at night; a sand bed of at least 5 cm is generally recommended. Not considered reef safe — has been reported to eat small invertebrates and harrass coral reef inhabitants, even if it does not directly nip coral tissue. Juveniles display a distinctly different blue-and-black coloration that transitions to vivid green with yellow crescent markings as the fish matures; juveniles are sometimes sold separately under their own descriptions. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals begin life as females and may transition to male. Wild adult males can reach up to 45 cm total length (per FishBase), though aquarium specimens commonly remain in the 25–30 cm range. Tank size recommendations varied across sources: LiveAquaria lists 130 gallons (~492 L), Reeflings lists 300 L — the larger value (500 L) was selected to accommodate this active, territorial species. Temperature ranges differed slightly: LiveAquaria gives 72–78°F (22–26°C) and FishBase/Reeflings give 24–28°C; a blended range of 23–27°C was used.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported as rare and is generally considered not achieved under typical aquarium conditions. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild, with dominant females transitioning to males.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Perciformes), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to Central Pacific, 1–20 m depth), wild maximum size (45 cm TL males), temperature (24–28°C), and protogynous hermaphroditism. Wild max size significantly exceeds aquarium-reported values; aquarium range of 20–30 cm used based on hobbyist sources.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.0–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), tank size (300 L), jumping behavior, sand-burrowing at night, and protogynous nature. Temperature range averaged with LiveAquaria to yield 23–27°C.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely if ever achieved in captivity. Protogynous hermaphrodite — females transition to males under social pressure.
